title: Why 'Little Women' Matters. Plus: Greta Gerwig! | The Big Picture
format: Amanda Dobbins takes the wheel to make the case for Greta Gerwig's 'Little Women,' walking Sean Fennessy (a total newcomer to the material) through the book's history and its many screen adaptations before they dig into the 2019 version scene by scene. Then Sean sits down with Gerwig herself to talk money, authorship, and the classic films she and DP Yorick Le Saux studied to build it.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: Greta Gerwig's adaptation, starring Saoirse Ronan, Florence Pugh, Emma Watson, Eliza Scanlen, Laura Dern, Meryl Streep, Timothée Chalamet, Bob Odenkirk, and Louis Garrel. Sean had never read the book or seen a prior version; Amanda has read it repeatedly and considers it one of her most anticipated films of the year. They cover Gerwig's split-timeline structure (young sisters intercut with the same actors ten years later), the framing device of Jo selling her story to Tracy Letts' publisher, the movie's preoccupation with money and female economic agency, and the performances: Ronan's Jo, Florence Pugh's much-rehabilitated Amy, Timothée Chalamet's Laurie, Eliza Scanlen's Beth, Chris Cooper, Laura Dern, and Meryl Streep. They debate the book-accurate ending (Jo marrying Professor Bhaer) and its awards chances after Golden Globes and SAG snubs.

films.title: Little Women (1933)
films.note: George Cukor's version, with Katharine Hepburn as Jo, a performance Amanda calls definitive to Hepburn's screen identity.

films.title: Little Women (1949)
films.note: Mervyn LeRoy's version, with Elizabeth Taylor as Amy and Janet Leigh as Meg.

films.title: Little Women (1994)
films.note: Gillian Armstrong's version, produced by Amy Pascal (also a producer on the 2019 film), with Winona Ryder as Jo, Christian Bale as Laurie, Claire Danes as Beth, Kirsten Dunst and Samantha Mathis splitting young and old Amy, and Susan Sarandon as Marmee. Amanda's formative version growing up; on a rewatch she found the other performances stronger than Ryder's, and calls it more conventionally sentimental than the 2019 film. Grossed $95 million and got three Oscar nominations, and is credited with kicking off the mid-'90s wave of Jane Austen adaptations.

films.title: Little Women
films.note: Gerwig wrote the script before directing 'Lady Bird' and had wanted to direct it since telling Amy Pascal she 'had to write it and direct it.' She talks about Virginia Woolf's 'A Room of One's Own,' Louisa May Alcott's own poverty and writing-for-survival, the opening pitch-meeting scene lifted nearly verbatim from the book, and building the ensemble with costume designer Jacqueline Durran, production designer Jess Gonchor, and DP Yorick Le Saux over two weeks of rehearsal.

films.title: Lady Bird
films.note: Gerwig's directorial debut; its commercial success (about $85 million) gave her the leverage to make 'Little Women' at a larger scale.

films.title: Beauty and the Beast
films.note: Costume designer Jacqueline Durran's more heightened work, cited as a contrast to her understated period pieces.

films.title: Mr. Turner
films.note: One of Durran's Mike Leigh collaborations, cited for its specific period costuming.

films.title: Another Year
films.note: Another Durran/Mike Leigh film, cited as barely looking costumed at all.

films.title: A Bigger Splash
films.note: A Luca Guadagnino film shot by DP Yorick Le Saux, part of why Gerwig wanted to work with him.

films.title: I Am Love
films.note: Another Guadagnino/Le Saux collaboration Gerwig calls stunning.

films.title: Carlos
films.note: Olivier Assayas's film, shot by Le Saux; Gerwig wanted the frenetic energy of this and the sensuousness of 'I Am Love' combined.

films.title: Two English Girls
films.note: A Truffaut touchstone for camera movement that 'never really feels its heaviness'; its device of characters addressing the camera also appears in 'Little Women.'

films.title: Jules and Jim
films.note: The other Truffaut reference point Gerwig mentions alongside 'Two English Girls.'

films.title: The Dead
films.note: John Huston's film, a 19th-century family chamber piece Gerwig and Le Saux studied on a tip from Wes Anderson.

films.title: Heaven's Gate
films.note: Studied for its waltz sequence, which Gerwig made the cast watch; she jokes her film didn't bankrupt a studio.

films.title: McCabe & Mrs. Miller
films.note: Referenced for its unglamorous, mottled period texture, including how the snow and ice look.

films.title: The Age of Innocence
films.note: Scorsese's film, which Gerwig realized drew on many of the same references she and Le Saux used; she singles out its opening party sequence.

films.title: The King of Comedy
films.note: A Scorsese flop-turned-favorite Gerwig and Noah Baumbach studied while making 'Mistress America.'

films.title: After Hours
films.note: The other Scorsese title Gerwig and Baumbach looked at for 'Mistress America.'

films.title: Mistress America
films.note: The Gerwig/Baumbach collaboration for which 'King of Comedy' and 'After Hours' were reference points.

films.title: Fanny and Alexander
films.note: Gerwig's aspirational double-feature pairing for 'Little Women,' when Sean asks.

films.title: Joker
films.note: The last great thing Gerwig had seen: a rainy-night 70mm screening in New York, where she also got to tell Todd Phillips how much she liked it.

Other: The Godfather Part II rewatchables episode
